item.title: Re: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. I like the crosslink, but the text is a little on the casual/colloquial side for my taste.

I prefer:

Further study into SCP-3223's potential relation to SCP-911 is ongoing.

And I've added it myself. Thanks Wikiwalk!

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: author made edits

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This SCP deals with ancient Egyptian mythology, specifically The Book of the Dead. SCP-911 is a copy of that book. I propose crosslinking them for that purpose.

Editing this:

The Book of the Dead is a magical and religious text intended to help the dead pass through challenges to become worthy of entering the afterlife.

To this:

The Book of the Dead is a magical and religious text intended to help the dead pass through challenges to become worthy of entering the afterlife. It is theorized that SCP-3223 may be related to SCP-911 in some way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think my tone might have come across as a bit cruel. I want you to know I was actually quite amazed by how well it's done

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wikipedia is a gateway to a lot of strange, wonderful trivia.

You should have named him Patrick and classified him as an eschatological entity, named the region of land it is allowed to tend "The Emerald Isle" and commissioned an MTF with the moniker "Why Isn't It Snakes". Once thar wa's done you could wrap it up by requesting a slot-swap with SCP-0317

I had to look up the reference to understand it >.>;

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Description: SCP-3223 is a collective designation referring to a humanoid entity (SCP-3223-1) and its anomalous entourage of incorporeal dung beetles (SCP-3223-2).

That collection of words in that order is so funny that the engramatic pattern that generated it should get an SCP designation

You should have named him Patrick and classified him as an eschatological entity, named the region of land it is allowed to tend "The Emerald Isle" and commissioned an MTF with the moniker "Why Isn't It Snakes". Once thar wa's done you could wrap it up by requesting a slot-swap with SCP-0317

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ghost Gandalf beetle gives you a little ghost beetle salute.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm a sucker for nods to Egyptian mythology and strangely wholesome Scips - the combination of the two gets an upvote from me!

item.title: Re: Author Post!
item.content:encoded: I have a dog named Gomez, But he responds to "Dumbass" as well…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
most people who look 'human' can be said to look like a certain race - someone might be accurately described as 'appears middle eastern' even if their family is greek, or mixed, or any other race.

Yeah, I'm not sure about adding in a detail on race just for the sake of doing so. I wanted to convey that there's something not quite human about this guy, and part of it is people not being able to attribute a race appearance-wise to him.

I like it fine as is.

Glad you did! Thanks for the upvote. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Judging from other human/oid SCP entries it's not unusual not to mention DNA test results. On the other hand that bit of the description is a bit odd. It didn't bother me at the time, but most people who look 'human' can be said to look like a certain race - someone might be accurately described as 'appears middle eastern' even if their family is greek, or mixed, or any other race. Maybe something like one of the following?

SCP-3223-1 appears to be a human of intermediate age. It's appearance is consistent with that of a non-anomalous inhabitant of northern Africa, with the exception that its eyes contain scarlet pupils.

Not sure of all the grammar/phrasing there works, but that's one way you could go with it. On the other hand, it didn't bother me as I read it so don't change it on my account. I like it fine as is.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can you provide an example of how to fix the issue?

Presumably DNA as well.

He's essentially the anomalous emissary of an ancient Egyptian deity, so… not really? I admit I didn't consider mentioning DNA, since the guy is supposed to be pretty otherworldly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3223-1 appears to be a human of indeterminate race and intermediate age

I can't upvote until this is fixed. He's got a skin color and height, right?

Presumably DNA as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for the kind comment! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I very much had a brief "why are you describing the humanoid's pupils and clothing -_-" moment that… I mostly stand by.

The scarlet pupils are a nod to the Egyptian deity Ra (who has a morning manifestation associated with him, which is Khepri, the scarab beetle who was believed to roll the sun up in the mornings like scarab beetles roll balls of dung), and a sort of tongue-in-cheek Easter egg regarding this: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eye_of_Ra

The simple clothing is to reference the fact that at some point in 3223's (original?) life, he was a priest. And he would have actually put mummified scarab beetles into the ears of the deceased who couldn't read the Book of the Dead.

eta: the fact that the clothing can't be removed is an indication that he's kind of stuck as a priest now. Maybe he's gotta mummify a certain number of pets or produce the perfect scarab guide to the afterlife before he can move on. You decide!

I love the book of the dead getting conflicted with all the dumb things we say to our pets. +1

I always wondered, "why were scarab beetles the only animals who could guide the dead?". It was probably the mythological significance and the fact that beetles could fit in human ears, but I like to think that maybe people's beloved pets were more interested in remembering their owners' words than the Book of the Dead, so scarabs were picked for the job.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very, very interesting concept, even to someone like me who knows next to nothing about Ancient Egyptian symbolism. It's good to know that there are always more creative ideas from creative people here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I very much had a brief "why are you describing the humanoid's pupils and clothing -_-" moment that… I mostly stand by. The irremovable clothing power is probably more silly than the understanding all languages thing.

He's barely the point, though. I love the book of the dead getting conflicted with all the dumb things we say to our pets. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the single cockroach is probably Sad Roach when he finally turned up following The Incident

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, I see. I don't know anything about the Book of the Dead (obviously.) I think the fact that they're just pretending to crawl on people and stuff could be made clearer, but maybe I was just being dense when I read it.

I wish I could have this guy send off my elderly cat when she goes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just showing up to say that my headcanon is that one of those spiders mummified was SCP-1470.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The pet scarab named Gandalf was a very satisfying climax to the entry

My personal headcanon is that the phrase Dr. Mercer used to greet the beetle every day was "YOU! SHALL HAVE! GRAAASSS!" /sets beetle loose in the lawn for exercise

I have visited the Rosicrucian Egyptian Museum in San Jose CA and seen the animal mummies.

Oh man. I've never seen an animal mummy before. Gotta add that to my bucket list!

I'm glad you liked the article. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
is there a reason for the beetles to be incorporeal?

Only ghost beetles can whisper spells from the Book of the Dead.

How does that even work with them crawling on people?

They just act like they're solid because it would probably not help an underworld traveler much if the beetle floated away doing its own thing or phased through objects instead of helping the person get to the afterlife.

Thanks for the +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is cool, but is there a reason for the beetles to be incorporeal? How does that even work with them crawling on people? Unless I missed something, I think that part should be taken out. +1 either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot. The pet scarab named Gandalf was a very satisfying climax to the entry.

I have visited the Rosicrucian Egyptian Museum in San Jose CA and seen the animal mummies. This brought back those eldritch, anomalous memories.
+1

item.title: Re: THE BEETLES
item.content:encoded:
Hahaha yes I changed the part about it crawling in your ear since this is not supposed to be a body horror article at all. (Although human mummification is pretty gross, all things considered.)

Don't make a colony out of them. MAKE A BAND AND NAME IT THE BEETLES

That might actually be feasible if SCP-3223-1 whispered Yellow Submarine lyrics to them or something.

Edit: wait, RENfish and HUXfish?

Ya. Their full names are Kylo Renfish and General Huxfish and I have a tumblr for their shenanigans I LOVE MY FISH SO MUCH DON'T JUDGE I do not yet have an Orson Krennicfish yet but maybe someday.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dung beetles are actually pretty cool if you don't focus too much on the dung part. The ones referred to as scarabs have a little crownlike feature on their heads! And Ancient Egyptians thought that every day the sun was pushed into the sky by a giant scarab and rolled away at night!

I am glad the article made you smile! :3

item.title: THE BEETLES
item.content:encoded:
OH MY GOD IT'S IN MY EAR GET IT OUT IT'S IN MY EAR

Oh wait…
You changed that part?
Fine. +1

Personal Recommendation: Don't make a colony out of them. MAKE A BAND AND NAME IT THE BEETLES

They make the sickest beets

Edit: wait, RENfish and HUXfish?

I smell something fishy here…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"anomalous entourage of incorporeal dung beetles" rates right up there with "succ" on the List of Things I Should Never Have to Read in a Positively Rated Article.

This was adorable, though. :D I just couldn't help smiling as I worked my way through the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Footnote 6 is probably the most fun I had writing a footnote ever for a mainlist SCP just because Gandalf. I'm glad you enjoyed the read!

/puts +1 on tiny hat, wears

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think there's an SCP that's literally the Book of the Dead, but I don't recall quite how that one goes.

That be SCP-911.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You had me at "anomalous entourage of incorporeal dung beetles".

I love Egyptian-derived stories, especially when those stories involve Gandalf, fatherly disappointment, and possible prospects of dung beetle orphanages. Have my + 1 and wear it on your hat.

item.title: Re: Author Post!
item.content:encoded:
Reminds me of my friend who calls her dog various nicknames involving "butt".

To be fair, I refer to my fish as butts sometimes. Especially Huxfish. He's such a prissy fish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think there's an SCP that's literally the Book of the Dead, but I don't recall quite how that one goes. Also CWW/Scanny's Egyptian sword/khopesh that has to do with the Ancient Egyptians believing that the heart controlled the body? And the temple that I think builds itself? Those are the ones that come to mind for me immediately. There's probably several more at least—Egyptian mythology is absolutely fascinating. (when i was in 6th grade one time my class went on a field trip to a small museum that ran CAT scans on mummies and the museum guide and i had a nice chat about the artifacts and random lore/mythology trivia and it was great)

Regarding the vocalizations—most of the "spells" are direct translations from the Wikipedia article on the spells from the Book of the Dead. There's some rather intriguing stuff mentioned in there regarding names to speak and the right things to say.

Thanks for the +1!

ETA: Also, thanks for helping me name the article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh, neat.

I'm trying to remember more SCP articles that tap into Egyptian Mythology as this one does, and nothing comes to mind, which is shocking as you'd think that be fertile ground for a host of SCPs. Maybe I just over looked them.

Either way, the idea of a Egyptian priest using what animals it can find as resources to continue the practice of its rites is unique and interesting. Additionally, the vocalization log is very entertaining, going from light creepy to humorous quite often.

+1. Job well done.

item.title: Re: Author Post!
item.content:encoded: If you're ever looking for more dialogue, I am contractually obligated to inform you that I call my cats "dumbass", sometimes "fucking dumbass", more than their real names.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
:D Thanks for reading it in chat! I really appreciate your time. :3

I picked the image because it looks like if you photoshopped some tiny cards in it would look like the beetle is playing Poker

that was my first thought upon seeing the image

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved it in chat, love it here. +1 wholeheartedly.

It is a pretty great image caption.

item.title: Author Post!
item.content:encoded:
Image from here: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Pretty_scarab_beetle_(21261230285).jpg
Licensed under Creative Commons Attribution-Share Alike 2.0 (CC BY-SA 2.0)

Big thanks to people (and pets) who contributed dialogue material for the logs:

  • Tuomey: (housemate’s) cat
  • kaktus: two cats
  • subletea: cockatiel
  • (Zyn has two fish that she taught to make kissy faces thank you Renfish thank you Huxfish)

And of course, many thanks to the reviewers in chat!

  • Gaffsey for wording help and flow and specific details!
  • Lazar for narrative development!
  • Flawed for beetle discussion!
  • WrongJohnSilver for overall commentary!
  • Synth_Synapses for reading!
  • Vacuum for nits and picks!
  • Sirens for reading and liking the image caption!
  • Photosynthetic for talk of resins!
  • ghostpage for random details!
Click to show background and rambling and stuff
PUT A SMALL BEETLE IN YOUR EAR

PUT A SCARAB BEETLE RIGHT INTO YOUR FAVORITE EAR

I read somewhere that if someone was too poor to afford a Book of the Dead in Ancient Egypt or couldn’t read, a priest could help them out by whispering spells from the Book to a scarab, killing and mummifying the scarab, and sticking that scarab in the dead person’s ear, so the ghost scarab could whisper the spells and answers to riddles to them and help them cross the underworld to get to the afterlife.

This person, uh… ran out of scarabs. So they use pets, because pets can identify with humans and sometimes help humans. But the pets don’t always consider magical spells to be the most important thing they’ve heard.

Why can the priest understand all languages but not talk? Probably got some beetles in their ears. Think Babel fish, kind of. But the beetles can only repeat things that they’ve been told.

All these things I looked up while writing!

  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scarab_(artifact)
  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scarabaeus_sacer
  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_Book_of_the_Dead_spells
  • http://news.nationalgeographic.com/news/2004/09/0915_040915_petmummies_2.html
  • https://www.wired.com/2013/11/egyptian-mummies-afterlife-diet/
  • https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Eye_of_Ra
  • https://www.reference.com/history/did-ancient-egyptian-priests-wear-48f7bfe894fd9da1
